analogy: Think of "Heat" through the chapter's own opening images: Acids, Bases and Salts 4 W e use in our daily life a large number of substances such as lemon, tamarind, common salt, sugar and vinegar. Let us recall tastes of some edible substances listed in Table 4.1. If you have not tasted any of... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 4.1 ACIDS AND BASES, CAUTION, ACIDS , BASES AND SALTS 39, 4.2 NATURAL I NDICATORS. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Heat is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that acids, Bases and Salts 4 W e use in our daily life a large number of substances such as lemon, tamarind, common salt, sugar and vinegar.

The text explains that let us recall tastes of some edible substances listed in Table 4.1.

The text explains that if you have not tasted any of these substances taste it now and enter the result in Table 4.1.

The text explains that you find that some of these substances taste sour, some taste bitter, some taste sweet and some taste salty.

The text explains that 4.1 ACIDS AND BASES Curd, lemon juice, orange juice and vinegar taste sour.

The text explains that these substances taste sour because they contain acids.

The text explains that the chemical nature of such substances is acidic.

The text explains that the word acid comes from the Latin word acere which means sour.

The text explains that the acids in these substances are natural acids.

The text explains that since, it does not taste sour it means, that it has no acids in it.

The text explains that if you rub its solution between fingers, it feels soapy.

The text explains that generally, substances like these which are bitter in taste and feel soapy on touching are known as bases.

The text explains that the nature of such substances is said to be basic.

The text explains that if we cannot taste every substance, how do we find its nature?

The text explains that special type of substances are used to test whether a substance is acidic or basic.

The text explains that the indicators change their colour when added to a solution containing an acidic or a basic substance.

The text explains that 4.4 Process of neutralisation Demonstrate the effect of the three indicators on each of these solutions.

The text explains that the evolved heat raises the temperature of the reaction mixture.

The text explains that salt and water are produced in this process with the evolution of heat.

The text explains that it helps us to digest food, as you have learnt in Chapter 2.

The text explains that name the source from which litmus solution is obtained.

The text explains that describe the process of neutralisation with the help of an example.

The text explains that explain why: (a) An antacid tablet is taken when you suffer from acidity.

The text explains that it controls every feature of the body such as our looks, colour of our eyes, our height etc.

The text explains that rub a slice of fresh beet root over the message.) 2.

The text explains that prepare red cabbage juice by boiling a piece of red cabbage in water.

The text explains that use it as an indicator and test the acidic and basic solutions with it.

The text explains that present your observations in the form of a table.

The text explains that bring the soil sample of your area, find out if it is acidic, basic or neutral.

The text explains that discuss with farmers if they treat the soil in any manner.

The text explains that find out the medicines, he prescribes to treat acidity.

The text explains that each cell in our body contains an acid, the deoxyribonucleic acid or DNA.

The text explains that proteins that build part of our cells are also made of amino acids.

Source: NCERT Class 7 Science, Chapter 4: Heat

concise_notes.definition: Acids, Bases and Salts 4 W e use in our daily life a large number of substances such as lemon, tamarind, common salt, sugar and vinegar.
CAUTION Great care should be taken while handling laboratory acids and bases because these are corrosive in nature, irritating and harmful to skin.
Acids, Bases and Salts 4 W e use in our daily life a large number of substances such as lemon, tamarind, common salt, sugar and vinegar.
The acids in these substances are natural acids.
4.1 (a) Lichens, and (b) Red and blue litmus paper (a) 4.2 NATURAL I NDICATORS AROUND US Litmus: A natural dye The most commonly used natural indicator is...
SCIENCE 40 Turmeric is another natural indicator Activity 4.2  Take a tablespoonful of turmeric powder.

revision_bullets: Acids, Bases and Salts 4 W e use in our daily life a large number of substances such as lemon, tamarind, common salt, sugar and vinegar. (NCERT Class 7 Science Ch4 pg 1)
Let us recall tastes of some edible substances listed in Table 4.1. (NCERT Class 7 Science Ch4 pg 1)
If you have not tasted any of these substances taste it now and enter the result in Table 4.1. (NCERT Class 7 Science Ch4 pg 1)
You find that some of these substances taste sour, some taste bitter, some taste sweet and some taste salty. (NCERT Class 7 Science Ch4 pg 1)
4.1 ACIDS AND BASES Curd, lemon juice, orange juice and vinegar taste sour. (NCERT Class 7 Science Ch4 pg 1)
These substances taste sour because they contain acids. (NCERT Class 7 Science Ch4 pg 1)
The word acid comes from the Latin word acere which means sour. (NCERT Class 7 Science Ch4 pg 1)
Since, it does not taste sour it means, that it has no acids in it. (NCERT Class 7 Science Ch4 pg 1)
If you rub its solution between fingers, it feels soapy. (NCERT Class 7 Science Ch4 pg 1)
Generally, substances like these which are bitter in taste and feel soapy on touching are known as bases. (NCERT Class 7 Science Ch4 pg 1)

prelims_traps: Do not treat 4.1 ACIDS AND BASES and CAUTION as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Heat; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Heat through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 4.1 ACIDS AND BASES and CAUTION to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
